agent_output: {"executive_summary":"This three-service architecture (Order → Payment → Notification) has critical synchronous coupling between Order and Payment, transitive dependency on Stripe, and a fire-and-forget notification path that silently drops emails. The failure mode enumeration reveals 9 distinct failure scenarios across 3 interaction boundaries. The resilience design applies Circuit Breaker, Retry with Backoff, Transactional Outbox, Timeout Budgets, and Idempotency Keys to bring the system from fragile to production-hardened.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Accepts customer orders, orchestrates payment charging, triggers notifications. Owns order lifecycle state machine (Created → PaymentPending → Paid → Confirmed → Failed).","boundaries":"Owns order aggregate. Must not leak payment details into its domain model. Responsible for its own resilience when calling downstream services."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Abstracts payment gateway interactions. Charges customers, manages payment state (Initiated → Charged → Failed → Refunded). Provides idempotent charge endpoint.","boundaries":"Owns payment aggregate. Encapsulates all Stripe-specific logic behind an anti-corruption layer. Never exposes Stripe tokens or objects to Order Service."},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Sends transactional emails (order confirmations, payment receipts, failure notices).","boundaries":"Owns notification templates and delivery state. Stateless from caller's perspective but must track delivery internally."},{"name":"Stripe (External Gateway)","responsibility":"Third-party payment processing. Outside our control — must be treated as unreliable by default.","boundaries":"External bounded context. All interactions go through Payment Service's ACL. Rate limits, downtime windows, and API versioning are Stripe's domain."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Order Service","to":"Payment Service","contract":"POST /payments/charge { orderId, amount, currency, idempotencyKey } → { paymentId, status, failureReason? }","coupling_type":"synchronous-temporal (high coupling — Order blocks on Payment response)"},{"from":"Payment Service","to":"Stripe","contract":"Stripe Charges API via SDK — synchronous HTTPS with Stripe-Idempotency-Key header","coupling_type":"synchronous-temporal + external (highest risk coupling in the system)"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"POST /notifications/send { type, orderId, recipientEmail, templateData } — fire-and-forget","coupling_type":"asynchronous-fire-and-forget (low coupling but zero delivery guarantee)"}],"data_flow":"Customer → Order Service (create order) → Payment Service (charge) → Stripe (process payment) → Payment Service (return result) → Order Service (update order state) → Notification Service (send confirmation). The critical path is Order→Payment→Stripe→Payment→Order. Notification is off the critical path but has no reliability guarantee.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Circuit Breaker","rationale":"Prevents cascade failure when Payment Service or Stripe is down. Fails fast instead of accumulating blocked threads and exhausting connection pools in Order Service."},{"pattern":"Retry with Exponential Backoff + Jitter","rationale":"Handles transient failures (network blips, 503s) on both Order→Payment and Payment→Stripe boundaries without creating thundering herd."},{"pattern":"Idempotency Keys","rationale":"Makes retries safe. Without idempotency, retrying a charge request risks double-charging customers — the single worst failure mode in a payment system."},{"pattern":"Timeout Budget (Deadline Propagation)","rationale":"Order Service sets an overall deadline. Payment Service inherits remaining budget for its Stripe call. Prevents unbounded waits and ensures the system fails within a predictable window."},{"pattern":"Transactional Outbox","rationale":"Replaces fire-and-forget HTTP for notifications. Order Service writes notification intent to an outbox table in the same transaction as the order state change. A relay process publishes to a message broker. Guarantees at-least-once delivery."},{"pattern":"Bulkhead (Connection Pool Isolation)","rationale":"Isolates Order Service's connection pool for Payment calls from its pool for Notification calls. A slow Notification Service cannot starve Payment connections."},{"pattern":"Fallback / Graceful Degradation","rationale":"When circuit is open on Payment, Order Service can accept orders in a 'pending payment' state and retry later, rather than rejecting the customer outright."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Context","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: orderId, states: Created/PaymentPending/Paid/Failed/Confirmed, invariant: cannot confirm unpaid order)","OutboxMessage (root: messageId, ensures at-least-once notification delivery)"]},{"name":"Payment Context","classification":"core","aggregates":["Payment (root: paymentId, states: Initiated/Charged/Failed/Refunded, invariant: charge is idempotent per idempotencyKey)"]},{"name":"Notification Context","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["Notification (root: notificationId, states: Pending/Sent/Failed, tracks delivery attempts)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Payment Context","downstream":"Order Context","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Payment defines the charge contract, Order conforms. Order must handle all Payment failure responses gracefully."},{"upstream":"Stripe (External)","downstream":"Payment Context","relationship":"Anti-Corruption Layer — Payment Service translates Stripe's API model into its own domain model. Stripe version changes do not propagate beyond Payment Service."},{"upstream":"Order Context","downstream":"Notification Context","relationship":"Customer-Supplier via Async Messaging — Order publishes domain events (OrderPaid, OrderFailed), Notification subscribes. Decoupled via outbox + broker."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Keep Order→Payment synchronous (with resilience patterns) rather than go fully async","gains":"Simpler user experience — customer gets immediate payment confirmation. Easier to reason about order state. No saga compensation logic needed for the happy path.","sacrifices":"Order Service availability is coupled to Payment Service availability. Must invest in circuit breaker, retries, and timeout budgets to compensate.","alternatives_considered":["Saga pattern with async payment — decouples availability but adds compensation complexity and eventual consistency that complicates the UX","Payment queue with polling — customer doesn't get immediate feedback, requires websocket/polling for status updates"]},{"decision":"Replace fire-and-forget notification with Transactional Outbox pattern","gains":"Guaranteed at-least-once delivery. No silent email drops. Notification failure is decoupled from order processing. Auditable outbox table.","sacrifices":"Added infrastructure complexity (outbox table, relay process or CDC, message broker). Slightly higher latency on notification delivery (seconds, not milliseconds).","alternatives_considered":["Keep fire-and-forget but add retry queue in Notification Service — still loses the initial HTTP call","Direct message broker publish from Order Service — not transactional with order state change, can lose messages on crash between DB commit and publish"]},{"decision":"Idempotency keys generated by Order Service (client-side)","gains":"Retries at any layer are safe. Order Service controls the idempotency scope. Double-submit from UI is also handled.","sacrifices":"Order Service must persist and manage idempotency keys. Adds a column/lookup to the order table.","alternatives_considered":["Server-side idempotency in Payment Service only — doesn't protect against Order Service retry generating new keys","Database unique constraint on (orderId, amount) — too coarse, doesn't handle legitimate re-charges after refund"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Payment Gateway","evolution_path":"Anti-corruption layer in Payment Service means Stripe can be swapped for Adyen, Braintree, or multi-gateway routing without touching Order Service. Add a GatewayAdapter interface behind the ACL."},{"area":"Notification Channel","evolution_path":"Outbox events carry notification intent, not channel-specific data. Notification Service can add SMS, push notifications, or webhooks without changing the upstream contract."},{"area":"Payment Retry Strategy","evolution_path":"Circuit breaker thresholds, retry counts, and backoff parameters should be externalized to configuration. Can tune in production without redeployment."},{"area":"Order Acceptance During Outages","evolution_path":"Fallback mode can evolve from simple rejection → queued retry → full saga pattern as business requirements grow. The order state machine already accommodates PaymentPending."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Synchronous Order→Payment coupling on the critical path","reason":"This is an intentional rigidity. The business requires immediate payment confirmation before order acceptance. Changing this to async fundamentally changes the user experience and requires saga compensation. Accept and harden, don't try to decouple."},{"area":"Idempotency key contract","reason":"Once clients depend on idempotency key semantics, changing the key generation strategy or scope is a breaking change. Define it right the first time."},{"area":"Order state machine transitions","reason":"The state machine (Created→PaymentPending→Paid→Confirmed / →Failed) enforces business invariants. Changes require careful migration of in-flight orders."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Double-charging customers due to retry without idempotency","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Idempotency keys generated by Order Service, passed through to Payment Service and forwarded to Stripe via Stripe-Idempotency-Key header. Payment Service also deduplicates on its side using (idempotencyKey) unique constraint."},{"risk":"Cascade failure: Stripe outage → Payment Service thread exhaustion → Order Service connection pool exhaustion → full system down","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Three-layer defense: (1) Timeout budgets — Order gives Payment 5s, Payment gives Stripe 3s. (2) Circuit breaker on Order→Payment with 50% error threshold over 10s window, 30s open duration. (3) Bulkhead — isolated thread/connection pools per downstream dependency."},{"risk":"Silent notification loss — customers never receive order confirmation emails","severity":"high","mitigation":"Replace fire-and-forget with Transactional Outbox. Monitor outbox lag. Alert if messages are not relayed within 60 seconds. Dead-letter queue for poison messages."},{"risk":"Stripe rate limiting (429) causing payment failures during traffic spikes","severity":"high","mitigation":"Payment Service implements adaptive rate limiting with token bucket. Retry 429s with Retry-After header value. Circuit breaker treats sustained 429s as open-circuit trigger. Consider queue-based load leveling for non-interactive payment retries."},{"risk":"Order stuck in PaymentPending state due to lost response (network partition after Stripe charges but before Payment Service receives response)","severity":"high","mitigation":"Payment Service reconciliation job polls Stripe for charges matching pending idempotency keys every 5 minutes. Resolves orphaned payments. Order Service has a TTL on PaymentPending — after 15 minutes, triggers reconciliation check before failing."},{"risk":"Partial failure: Payment succeeds but Order Service crashes before persisting Paid state","severity":"high","mitigation":"Idempotency keys allow safe re-charge attempt on Order Service recovery — Stripe and Payment Service return the original successful charge. Order Service startup recovery scans for PaymentPending orders older than timeout and reconciles."},{"risk":"Notification Service backpressure causing Order Service thread blocking on fire-and-forget calls (even async HTTP has connection limits)","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Transactional Outbox completely eliminates this risk — Order Service never makes HTTP calls to Notification Service. Relay process handles backpressure independently."},{"risk":"Clock skew or network latency causing timeout budget miscalculation","severity":"low","mitigation":"Use remaining-budget headers rather than absolute timestamps. Add buffer margin (e.g., reserve 500ms for network overhead). Monitor p99 latencies and adjust budgets quarterly."},{"risk":"Message broker failure preventing outbox relay","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Outbox relay retries with backoff. Messages accumulate in outbox table (durable by design). Alert on outbox depth > threshold. Broker should be clustered for HA."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase the implementation in three waves. Wave 1 (Critical Safety): Add idempotency keys end-to-end and timeout budgets — this prevents the worst failure mode (double-charging) immediately. Wave 2 (Cascade Protection): Add circuit breaker on Order→Payment boundary and bulkhead isolation. Wave 3 (Notification Reliability): Implement transactional outbox, deploy relay process, add message broker, migrate away from fire-and-forget.","files_to_create_or_modify":["order-service/src/clients/PaymentClient.{ts,java} — Add circuit breaker wrapper, timeout config, idempotency key generation, retry logic","order-service/src/models/Order.{ts,java} — Add idempotencyKey field, PaymentPending state with TTL","order-service/src/models/OutboxMessage.{ts,java} — New entity for transactional outbox","order-service/src/jobs/OutboxRelay.{ts,java} — Polls outbox table, publishes to broker, marks relayed","order-service/src/jobs/OrderReconciliation.{ts,java} — Scans stale PaymentPending orders, triggers reconciliation","payment-service/src/controllers/ChargeController.{ts,java} — Add idempotency key deduplication, Stripe-Idempotency-Key forwarding","payment-service/src/clients/StripeAdapter.{ts,java} — ACL wrapping Stripe SDK with timeout, retry, circuit breaker","payment-service/src/jobs/PaymentReconciliation.{ts,java} — Polls Stripe for orphaned charges","notification-service/src/consumers/NotificationConsumer.{ts,java} — Subscribe to broker instead of HTTP endpoint","infrastructure/docker-compose.yml or k8s manifests — Add message broker (RabbitMQ/Kafka)","shared/config/resilience.{yaml,json} — Externalized circuit breaker thresholds, timeout budgets, retry policies"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use a resilience library (Resilience4j for Java, cockatiel/opossum for Node.js, Polly for .NET) rather than hand-rolling circuit breakers. Wrap all external calls in a resilience pipeline: Timeout → Retry → Circuit Breaker → Bulkhead (outermost to innermost). Idempotency keys should be UUIDv4 generated once per order attempt and stored with the order. Outbox pattern: write message row in same DB transaction as business state change, relay process polls with SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Never retry non-idempotent operations — ensure idempotency keys are in place BEFORE enabling retries","Do not retry 4xx errors (except 429) — they are permanent failures, retrying wastes resources and may trigger rate limits","Circuit breaker must measure errors at the right granularity — per-endpoint, not per-host — a failing /charge endpoint should not open the circuit for /refund","Do not use distributed transactions (2PC) across services — they don't scale and create tight coupling. Use idempotency + reconciliation instead","Outbox relay must handle duplicate delivery (at-least-once) — Notification Service must be idempotent on (notificationId)","Do not set aggressive timeouts without measuring baseline latencies first — start with p99 + 50% margin","Fire-and-forget async HTTP is NOT the same as reliable async messaging — it gives the illusion of decoupling while silently dropping failures"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":92,"high_confidence_areas":["Failure mode enumeration — these are well-documented distributed systems failure patterns","Idempotency key design — industry standard for payment systems","Circuit breaker and timeout budget patterns — proven at scale","Transactional outbox replacing fire-and-forget — clearly superior for notification reliability"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific timeout values (5s, 3s) — would need production latency data to tune accurately","Circuit breaker thresholds (50% over 10s) — starting points that need empirical tuning","Whether the business can tolerate a fallback 'accept order, charge later' mode — requires product input"],"assumptions":["The system uses a relational database that supports transactions (for outbox pattern)","Stripe is the only payment gateway currently (single adapter behind ACL)","Order confirmation email is the only notification type currently","Traffic volume does not require queue-based load leveling on the payment path today","The team has operational capacity to run a message broker in production"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"This three-service architecture (Order → Payment → Notification) has critical synchronous coupling between Order and Payment, transitive dependency on Stripe, and a fire-and-forget notification path that silently drops emails. The failure mode enumeration reveals 9 distinct failure scenarios across 3 interaction boundaries. The resilience design applies Circuit Breaker, Retry with Backoff, Transactional Outbox, Timeout Budgets, and Idempotency Keys to bring the system from
